ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'chicken curry' class (FOODON_03301702). No specific Kundapuri or Mangalorean coconut chicken curry class exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chicken meat dishes broadly. No Kundapuri-specific code exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Kori Kundapuri or Kundapuri chicken curry specifically.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kori Kundapuri is approximately 300 g, comprising boneless chicken pieces in a rich coconut-based curry sauce, served with neer dosa or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, homemade, with coconut milk – ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), adapted for Kundapuri-style coconut chicken curry
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for chicken curry with coconut. Kori Kundapuri is a coconut-based boneless chicken curry from coastal Karnataka. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN coconut chicken curry entry, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for chicken curry with coconut milk (FDC ID 2097270) to confirm plausibility. Serving size set at 300 g based on typical restaurant portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes with coconut – analogous estimate based on chicken curry with coconut milk entries; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kori Kundapuri in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous coconut chicken curry dishes in the database, which report GI values in the range 30–45 due to low net carbohydrate content, high protein, and high fat from coconut. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (35 × 3.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 4.0 g. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for protein- and fat-dominant curry dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Kori Kundapuri provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing nearly 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low net carbohydrate content (~3.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kori Kundapuri has a low estimated GI of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 4 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich, low-glycaemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is paired with low-GI staples such as cauliflower rice or a small portion of basmati rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut (~5.8 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut base contributes approximately 5.8 g saturated fat per 100 g, which may raise LDL cholesterol with frequent consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g and favour preparations using reduced coconut or light coconut milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kori Kundapuri (also known as Kundapuri Chicken Curry or Kundapur Chicken Curry) is a traditional boneless chicken curry originating from Kundapura, a coastal town in Karnataka, South India. The dish is characterised by a rich, aromatic gravy made from freshly grated or desiccated coconut, dried red chillies, fresh coriander leaves, onion, garlic, ginger, turmeric, coriander seeds, cumin, black pepper, and tamarind, slow-cooked together to develop a deep, complex flavour profile. The coconut base gives the curry a creamy, slightly sweet undertone balanced by the heat of red chillies and the tang of tamarind, while fresh coriander adds brightness. The texture is thick and coating, with tender boneless chicken pieces absorbing the spiced coconut sauce. Kori Kundapuri is a staple of Mangalorean and Tulu Nadu cuisine and is traditionally served with neer dosa (thin rice crepes), kori rotti (crispy rice wafers), or steamed rice. Nutritionally, it is a protein-rich dish (~13.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat from coconut (~9.2 g per 100 g, predominantly saturated), low carbohydrate (~3.8 g per 100 g), and a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (~380 mg per 100 g), warranting attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is commonly prepared as halal. In Singapore, Kori Kundapuri appears on menus at South Indian and North Indian restaurants serving coastal Karnataka specialities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kori Kundapuri?
Is Kori Kundapuri good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Kori Gassi and Kori Kundapuri?
What does Kori Kundapuri taste like?
What is Kundapur chicken curry served with?

confidence_reason: Kori Kundapuri is a well-documented regional dish from coastal Karnataka. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN coconut chicken curry data, which is the closest approved primary source for Indian dishes of this type. No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Kori Kundapuri exists; adaptation is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable coconut chicken curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact match exists in any approved primary source.
